Really disappointing all around.

Us, being their earthly descendants we are all vulnerable to temptation to sin and have sinned (sin: which is a moral flaw/crime against God). In the story of Moses, God gives what is called the moral law(the Ten Commandments) the Bible says “the wages of sin is death” breaking these laws we are punished by death/hell. We’ve all broken at least one of these commandments. So God sends Jesus(the son of God) who lives a perfect and sinless life. Jesus offers himself as a sacrifice in place of the punishment that we deserve for breaking the Moral Law. So right before Jesus passes away on the cross, he says “it is finished” which means the punishment for our sins was fulfilled. Then Jesus dies. He rises three days later, signifying he had defeated death, which is the wages for sin.   (John 3:16) sums up why God offered his Son as a sacrifice for our sin.


How: Romans 10:9 “that if you confess with your mouth the Lord Jesus and believe in your heart that God has raised Him from the dead, you will be saved.”

Ask God for forgiveness, repent and ask him into your Heart and Life in the name of Jesus. Repent: to feel such regret for sins or crimes as produces amendment of life, to be sorry, regretful.

When you do this God will begin to make changes in your life, this means you are “born again” When you actually pray and try to get rid of sin in your life, you’ll start to see a change. This process will last throughout your whole if you are really committed. God knows our hearts and intentions better then we know ourselves. I hope this is a major blessing. I hope you really take into account what I said and really pray and think on these things.

Dude, it's not that serious. Watching - even writing ≠ coveting a real life equivalent or something, lol. That's like saying anyone who enjoys a good martial arts match wants to beat the sh*t out of the world.

@mariesummers. Enjoying a contest of consensual fighters is different than enjoying suffering, victimization, violence, and gore. Even if it's fiction. You're still indulging in the same darkness. You've just excused it because there wasn't a real victim. If someone enjoys real suffering there are two evil things, if you enjoy fake suffering there's still one evil thing.

@vatofat - Sooo... Even in the case of media with a message meant to address and bring awareness to or discourage the fostering of real life issues/possible future trajectories, it's evil? That just sounds like a lack of nuance to me, not a reasonable rule.

I don't particularly care for the franchise, but this series has actually been a grounds to talk about rather serious topics (whether one agrees on their stance or not) by use of exaggerated displays of real world systems and ideologies.

Dystopian/Utopian societies in fiction, for example, are largely used to showcase current, past or potential future problems the world either must learn from, change or prevent, and they do so by taking the aspect of focus and blowing it up into an undeniable terror. So if you read, say, The Hunger Games, which had strong themes concerning subjects like exploitation, hubris, avarice, entertainment as distraction, desensitization, and glorification of killing/death, is it a problem to greatly appreciate the book for what it set out to speak against and how well it managed to get its themes to resonate with audiences? That series is one giant spill of words against these problems, which is shown very strongly by the fact that

*SPOILER*

Katniss kills Coin because the idea of punishing the Capitol by forcing them to face the games is just as cruel, and if Coin would've lived, she'd have caused generations of the same misery. Katniss underscored the story's message in stopping Coin despite how controversial it might be and what people may think of her, because it was worth ending generational pain and suffering.

I'm not trying to be rude, but I just think this is an incredibly surface level argument for mediums showing violence that can be far more diverse and deep. Not every medium covering or showing violence is negative, and there are far too many minds out there to demonize one form of getting a message across over another, because not everyone will take in information the same as you. Plenty of films with violence or disturbing imagery have had incredibly positive, lasting effects around the world.

Aside of that, I personally find this take incredibly serious in general. There's a difference between watching a violent film and watching pure torture porn. It's not really disgusting to me until the violence is gratuitous and therefore wholly unnecessary to tell the story. I'm not mad if a crime fic shows a horrible crime to give relevant info, I'm mad if they spend ten minutes showing someone writhing in agony despite me getting the same message from a cutaway where they look disheveled in the next scene. Lots of these stories showcase human strengths, hope, camaraderie, empathy, triumph + more, and give a less painful or direct way for people to process fear, anger, loss, etc. Your pov seriously lacks nuance, since even movies that have violence can be vehicles to parallel and therefore bring awareness and opposition to real world problems.

@mariesummers. No one who watches these movies is interested in a social commentary on the dangers of a dystopia. There are better offerings than this, 1984, Elysium, and even hunger games (though it too whet viewers blood lust). The nuance is when you can tell the difference between examples in a genre, and what motivates their exists in the first place. All that business about social and cultural commentary is what people say when they don't want to admit that they have blood lust. I know what's in a persons heart by what they do and say. We all think no one else can know that stuff about ourselves, but we all judge others on that basis alone. We're not that big of a mystery. These movies are blood lust only. There are many other movies that are too, and many that are not. Maybe we're all too used to watching people die to realize it anymore.

@vatofat - I don't look at a bad romance movie that people happen to enjoy and say no one watches it for the romance because if they wanted romance, there are better ones out there. Same with comedy. Or sci-fi. Or literally anything.

Besides, it's a little odd to assume that there is definitely no one who ever watches these films for the commentary. Plenty of people aren't here for the commentary just like plenty of people either are or come for both, and to assume anyone who says they want to see how different movies handle commentary on various topics just to hide a blood lust is just letting confirmation bias steer what you see and accept (esp when you consider some watch for discussion/critique, and critique isn't about salivating over media, but improving upon or shedding light on what a story did right and wrong, so it's good to have people who give fair shots to each thing as an individual piece and explore what did and didn't work).

Sure, people aren't always a giant enigma, but they're a bigger mystery that you're giving credit for; that is, you don't know people around the entire globe nearly that well to make a blanket statement for everyone. It's not groundbreaking that multiple people probably watch the film for a multitude of different reasons with some overlap. Plus, even in the case of a bad film, not everyone's shutting their brains off and accepting what they saw. Some films tackle themes that are far too important to not see handled right, and/or are just outright questionable (like Cuties), and it's important to have people that watch films to see that when a topic is addressed it's either done right or - for people who aren't privy - they understand it was handled poorly. Cuties was defended by so many people, thank *goodness* there were people who watched the film and trailer to call out bull claims of what it was "commenting" on and stop it from being further glorified.

Seriously, this whole black and white view of everything doesn't work when it's founded on presumptuous statements like how you "know what's in a person's heart" by things like watching a movie, as though that can tell you the motivations for doing so and anything about their character.

I mean, I'll just have to disagree and let it be on this one. We probably won't come to a mutual agreement here.
